final_proof:
We are given \(f:\mathbb{R}_{>0}\to\mathbb{R}_{>0}\) satisfying  
\[
\sqrt{\frac{x^{2}+f(y)^{2}}{2}}\ge\frac{f(x)+y}{2}\ge\sqrt{x\,f(y)}\qquad\forall x,y>0.
\]
Squaring the inequalities yields  
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(f(x)+y)^{2}\le 2\bigl(x^{2}+f(y)^{2}\bigr) \tag{1}\\[2mm]
&f(x)+y\ge 2\sqrt{x\,f(y)} \tag{2}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \(x,y>0\).

---
### 1.  Injectivity of \(f\)

Assume \(f(x_{1})=f(x_{2})=c\).  From (1) with \((x_{1},x_{2})\) and \((x_{2},x_{1})\) we obtain  
\[
(c+x_{2})^{2}\le 2(x_{1}^{2}+c^{2}),\qquad (c+x_{1})^{2}\le 2(x_{2}^{2}+c^{2}).
\]
Subtracting the second inequality from the first gives  
\[
(c+x_{2})^{2}-(c+x_{1})^{2}\le 2(x_{1}^{2}-x_{2}^{2}).
\]
The left‑hand side equals \((x_{2}-x_{1})(2c+x_{1}+x_{2})\); the right‑hand side equals \(-2(x_{2}-x_{1})(x_{1}+x_{2})\).  Hence  
\[
(x_{2}-x_{1})(2c+x_{1}+x_{2})+2(x_{2}-x_{1})(x_{1}+x_{2})\le0,
\]
i.e.  
\[
(x_{2}-x_{1})(2c+3x_{1}+3x_{2})\le0.
\]
Because \(2c+3x_{1}+3x_{2}>0\), we must have \(x_{2}\le x_{1}\).  Subtracting the first inequality from the second gives similarly \(x_{1}\le x_{2}\).  Therefore \(x_{1}=x_{2}\); thus \(f\) is injective.

---
### 2.  Proof that \(f(x)<2x\) for all \(x\)

Suppose, for a contradiction, that there exists \(x_{0}>0\) with \(f(x_{0})\ge 2x_{0}\).

Apply (2) with \((x_{0},f(x_{0}))\):
\[
f(x_{0})+f(x_{0})\ge2\sqrt{x_{0}\,f(f(x_{0}))}\;\Longrightarrow\; f(x_{0})\ge\sqrt{x_{0}\,f(f(x_{0}))}
\;\Longrightarrow\; f(x_{0})^{2}\ge x_{0}\,f(f(x_{0})). \tag{3}
\]

Apply (1) with \((x_{0},f(x_{0}))\):
\[
(2f(x_{0}))^{2}\le 2\bigl(x_{0}^{2}+f(f(x_{0}))^{2}\bigr)\;\Longrightarrow\; 4f(x_{0})^{2}\le 2x_{0}^{2}+2f(f(x_{0}))^{2}
\;\Longrightarrow\; 2f(x_{0})^{2}\le x_{0}^{2}+f(f(x_{0}))^{2}. \tag{4}
\]

Apply (2) with \((f(x_{0}),x_{0})\):
\[
f(f(x_{0}))+x_{0}\ge 2\sqrt{f(x_{0})\,f(x_{0})}=2f(x_{0})\;\Longrightarrow\; f(f(x_{0}))\ge 2f(x_{0})-x_{0}. \tag{5}
\]

Apply (1) with \((f(x_{0}),x_{0})\):
\[
\bigl(f(f(x_{0}))+x_{0}\bigr)^{2}\le 2\bigl(f(x_{0})^{2}+f(x_{0})^{2}\bigr)=4f(x_{0})^{2}
\;\Longrightarrow\; f(f(x_{0}))+x_{0}\le 2f(x_{0})\;\Longrightarrow\; f(f(x_{0}))\le 2f(x_{0})-x_{0}. \tag{6}
\]

From (5) and (6) we obtain  
\[
f(f(x_{0}))=2f(x_{0})-x_{0}. \tag{7}
\]

Now apply (1) with \((x_{0},f(f(x_{0})))\).  Using (7),
\[
\bigl(f(x_{0})+f(f(x_{0}))\bigr)^{2}\le 2\bigl(x_{0}^{2}+f(f(x_{0}))^{2}\bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\; \bigl(f(x_{0})+2f(x_{0})-x_{0}\bigr)^{2}\le 2\bigl(x_{0}^{2}+(2f(x_{0})-x_{0})^{2}\bigr).
\]
The left‑hand side is \((3f(x_{0})-x_{0})^{2}\); the right‑hand side expands to  
\[
2\bigl(x_{0}^{2}+4f(x_{0})^{2}-4x_{0}f(x_{0})+x_{0}^{2}\bigr)=8f(x_{0})^{2}-8x_{0}f(x_{0})+4x_{0}^{2}.
\]
Thus  
\[
9f(x_{0})^{2}-6x_{0}f(x_{0})+x_{0}^{2}\le 8f(x_{0})^{2}-8x_{0}f(x_{0})+4x_{0}^{2},
\]
which simplifies to  
\[
f(x_{0})^{2}+2x_{0}f(x_{0})-3x_{0}^{2}\le0\;\Longleftrightarrow\;(f(x_{0})+3x_{0})(f(x_{0})-x_{0})\le0.
\]
Since \(f(x_{0})+3x_{0}>0\), we must have \(f(x_{0})\le x_{0}\), contradicting \(f(x_{0})\ge 2x_{0}\).  Therefore no such \(x_{0}\) exists; hence  
\[
\boxed{f(x)<2x\qquad\forall x>0.} \tag{8}
\]

---
### 3.  A functional equation

Fix \(x>0\) and set \(y=2x-f(x)\).  By (8), \(y>0\).  From (2):
\[
f(x)+y\ge 2\sqrt{x\,f(y)}\;\Longrightarrow\; 2x\ge 2\sqrt{x\,f(y)}\;\Longrightarrow\; x\ge\sqrt{x\,f(y)}\;\Longrightarrow\; f(y)\le x.
\]
From (1):
\[
(f(x)+y)^{2}\le 2\bigl(x^{2}+f(y)^{2}\bigr)\;\Longrightarrow\; 4x^{2}\le 2\bigl(x^{2}+f(y)^{2}\bigr)\;\Longrightarrow\; 2x^{2}\le x^{2}+f(y)^{2}\;\Longrightarrow\; f(y)\ge x.
\]
Hence \(f(y)=x\).  Consequently  
\[
\boxed{f\bigl(2x-f(x)\bigr)=x\qquad\forall x>0.} \tag{9}
\]

---
### 4.  Proof that \(f(x)\le x\) for all \(x\)

Assume, for contradiction, that there exists \(x_{0}>0\) with \(f(x_{0})>x_{0}\).  Define a sequence \((x_{n})\) by \(x_{n+1}=2x_{n}-f(x_{n})\).  Because of (8), each \(x_{n}\) is positive.  From (9) we have \(f(x_{n+1})=x_{n}\).  Then  
\[
f(x_{n+1})-x_{n+1}=x_{n}-(2x_{n}-f(x_{n}))=f(x_{n})-x_{n},
\]
so the difference \(d:=f(x_{n})-x_{n}\) is constant for all \(n\).  Thus \(d>0\) and \(f(x_{n})=x_{n}+d\).  Then  
\[
x_{n+1}=2x_{n}-(x_{n}+d)=x_{n}-d.
\]
Hence \(x_{n}=x_{0}-nd\).  For \(n=\lfloor x_{0}/d\rfloor+1\) we obtain \(x_{n}\le0\), contradicting \(x_{n}>0\).  Therefore no such \(x_{0}\) exists; we have  
\[
\boxed{f(x)\le x\qquad\forall x>0.} \tag{10}
\]

---
### 5.  Proof that \(f(x)\ge x\) for all \(x\)

From (9) every \(x\) belongs to the image of \(f\); hence \(f\) is surjective.  Together with injectivity (Section 1) it is bijective, so an inverse \(f^{-1}\) exists.  Equation (9) gives  
\[
f^{-1}(x)=2x-f(x). \tag{11}
\]
Moreover, \(f^{-1}\) satisfies the same functional equation:
\[
f^{-1}\bigl(2y-f^{-1}(y)\bigr)=f^{-1}\bigl(2y-(2y-f(y))\bigr)=f^{-1}(f(y))=y.
\]

Now suppose there exists \(y_{0}>0\) with \(f^{-1}(y_{0})>y_{0}\).  Let \(d=f^{-1}(y_{0})-y_{0}>0\) and define \(y_{n+1}=2y_{n}-f^{-1}(y_{n})\).  By (11), \(f^{-1}(y_{n+1})=y_{n}\), and the same calculation as in Section 4 shows that the difference \(f^{-1}(y_{n})-y_{n}\) is constant.  Therefore \(f^{-1}(y_{n})=y_{n}+d\) for all \(n\), whence \(y_{n+1}=y_{n}-d\).  Consequently \(y_{n}=y_{0}-nd\).  For sufficiently large \(n\) we have \(y_{n}\le0\), contradicting the fact that the domain of \(f^{-1}\) is \(\mathbb{R}_{>0}\).  Hence \(f^{-1}(y)\le y\) for every \(y\).  Using (11),
\[
2y-f(y)=f^{-1}(y)\le y\;\Longrightarrow\; f(y)\ge y\qquad\forall y>0. \tag{12}
\]

---
### 6.  Conclusion

From (10) and (12) we obtain \(f(x)=x\) for all \(x>0\).  It is straightforward to verify that \(f(x)=x\) satisfies the original inequalities (by AM‑GM and \((x-y)^{2}\ge0\)).  Hence the only function is  
\[
\boxed{f(x)=x}.
\]
